sections.plain_summary: Salicylate intolerance is a sensitivity to salicylates, naturally occurring compounds found in many plant-based foods. People with this intolerance experience symptoms like headaches, digestive issues, skin reactions, or mood changes after eating high-salicylate foods. It is not a true allergy but rather a reaction related to how the body processes these compounds.
sections.detailed_summary: Salicylate intolerance occurs when the body has difficulty metabolising salicylates—plant-derived compounds with anti-inflammatory properties present in fruits, vegetables, spices, and processed foods. The condition is thought to involve impaired activity of the enzyme phenolsulfotransferase (PST) or other detoxification pathways, though the exact mechanism is not fully understood. Symptoms can be delayed and dose-dependent, developing hours or days after consuming high-salicylate foods. The condition is distinct from salicylate allergy (which triggers immune responses) and is often grouped with other food sensitivities affecting histamine or amine metabolism. Management typically involves identifying and avoiding trigger foods, though the threshold varies widely between individuals.

prevalence.summary: The exact prevalence is uncertain, but salicylate intolerance is thought to affect a small minority of the population, with higher rates among people with existing digestive or neurological conditions.
prevalence.estimate: estimated 0.5–3% of the general population, possibly higher in people with irritable bowel syndrome, autism, or ADHD
prevalence.notes: Prevalence data is limited because there is no gold-standard diagnostic test and symptom overlap with other conditions makes epidemiology difficult. The condition is better-recognised in Australia and New Zealand. Many cases may go undiagnosed or be misattributed to other food intolerances. Gender and age distributions are not well-characterised.

subgroups.name: Digestive-predominant salicylate intolerance
subgroups.description: Symptoms primarily affect the gastrointestinal tract, with minimal neurological or behavioural effects.
distinguishing_features: Bloating, gas, diarrhoea, or constipation triggered by specific foods
May overlap significantly with irritable bowel syndrome
Response to low-salicylate diet is often clear and reproducible

subgroups.name: Neurological/behavioural salicylate intolerance
subgroups.description: Symptoms centre on headaches, mood changes, sleep disturbance, or behavioural dysregulation, often with minimal GI involvement.
distinguishing_features: Irritability, anxiety, hyperactivity, or emotional volatility
More commonly reported in children with ADHD or autism

subgroups.name: Mixed or multi-system salicylate intolerance
subgroups.description: Symptoms span digestive, neurological, and skin systems, often with variable intensity.
distinguishing_features: Combination of GI upset, headaches, and skin reactions
Symptoms may cluster or vary depending on food quantity and type
May coexist with other food sensitivities (histamine, amines)

symptoms.notes: Delayed onset (2–48 hours after eating), severity varies with food quantity, often worse in afternoon or evening

symptoms.symptom: Brain fog, poor concentration, or memory difficulty
symptoms.notes: Often described as haziness or mental fatigue; may appear within hours of trigger foods

symptoms.symptom: Irritability, mood swings, or emotional dysregulation
symptoms.notes: Particularly noted in children; can mimic ADHD or anxiety symptoms
More common in children; may worsen after eating certain fruits or coloured foods
May manifest as difficulty falling asleep or fragmented sleep; often follows high-salicylate meals
Occurs within hours of eating; may be accompanied by abdominal discomfort
Often watery; can be delayed by 6–24 hours after trigger consumption
Less common than diarrhoea but may occur in some individuals
Usually mild to moderate; may be diffuse or colicky
Often triggered by high-salicylate fruits; may be delayed by hours or days
Less common; may occur with direct contact with high-salicylate foods
Rare but reported; may accompany digestive or skin symptoms
May occur shortly after eating; similar to histamine-mediated flushing

key_patterns: Symptoms appear consistently 2–48 hours after eating specific foods (fruits, spices, processed foods)
Multiple symptom types across different body systems (digestive + neurological + skin)
Symptoms worsen with seasonal fruits (stone fruits, berries) or strongly flavoured foods
Improvement occurs within days to weeks when suspected high-salicylate foods are avoided
Symptoms are dose-dependent: small amounts may not trigger reactions, but larger portions do
Other family members have similar sensitivities or food intolerances

at_home.name: Elimination diet (low-salicylate trial)
at_home.description: Restrict intake to low-salicylate foods (peeled potato, white rice, chicken, beef, pear, lettuce, cabbage, green beans) for 3–5 days and monitor symptoms. If symptoms improve significantly, salicylate intolerance is likely. Avoid dietary supplements and processed foods during this period.
at_home.safety_notes: This diet is nutritionally incomplete and should not be continued long-term. If you have diabetes, eating disorders, or are pregnant, consult a doctor or dietitian before starting. Stop immediately if symptoms worsen or new symptoms appear.

at_home.name: Structured food and symptom diary
at_home.description: Record all foods and drinks consumed, times of consumption, and any symptoms (type, onset time, duration, severity) for at least 2 weeks. Use a scale (e.g. 1–10) for symptom severity. Look for temporal patterns linking specific foods to symptom clusters.
at_home.safety_notes: This is observational only and subject to recall bias and psychological expectation. Patterns identified should be confirmed by reintroduction testing before concluding salicylate intolerance.

at_home.name: Single-food reintroduction
at_home.description: After a symptom-free period on a low-salicylate diet, reintroduce one suspected high-salicylate food (e.g. a strawberry, a teaspoon of tomato paste, or a small spice amount) and observe for 24–48 hours. Record any symptom recurrence. Wait at least 3 days between reintroductions.
at_home.safety_notes: Reintroduction may trigger symptoms; have any needed symptom relief on hand. If severe reactions occur (anaphylaxis, severe swelling), seek emergency care immediately. Psychological expectation can influence results; consider blind testing if possible.

clinical.name: Symptom history and dietary assessment
clinical.description: A healthcare provider takes a detailed history of symptom onset, types, timing, and relationship to specific foods. A dietitian may conduct a structured food history and assess nutritional intake. This is the primary diagnostic method and relies on clinical pattern recognition rather than objective markers.
clinical.availability: Available through primary care physicians, registered dietitians, or gastroenterologists. Increasingly offered by functional and integrative medicine practitioners.

clinical.name: Supervised elimination and reintroduction diet
clinical.description: A registered dietitian or doctor supervises a structured low-salicylate elimination diet, monitors symptoms, and systematically reintroduces foods to confirm triggers. More rigorous than home testing and ensures adequate nutrition during the trial.
clinical.availability: Provided by registered dietitians (often through referral from a GP or specialist) or in specialist allergy or gastroenterology clinics.

clinical.name: Blood tests for PST enzyme activity
clinical.description: Measurement of phenolsulfotransferase (PST) activity in blood. Low PST activity is theoretically linked to salicylate intolerance, but the test is not standardised and its clinical utility is unproven. Not widely available.
clinical.availability: Not routinely available in mainstream clinical labs. May be offered by some functional medicine or research laboratories. Interpretation is unclear and not yet evidence-based for diagnosis.

clinical.name: Skin prick testing or IgE serology
clinical.description: Tests for immunological allergy (IgE-mediated) to salicylates or salicylate-rich foods. Negative results help rule out true allergy and support intolerance diagnosis. Does not measure intolerance itself.
clinical.availability: Available through allergists or immunologists. Useful for ruling out classic food allergy but does not confirm salicylate intolerance.

clinical.name: Exclusion of other conditions (coeliac, IBS, FODMAP intolerance, histamine intolerance)
clinical.description: Blood tests for tissue transglutaminase (tTG) antibodies and total IgA (coeliac serology), hydrogen breath test (FODMAP intolerance), or symptom trials ruling out histamine intolerance. Helps differentiate from other common food sensitivities with overlapping symptoms.
clinical.availability: Available through primary care and gastroenterology. Often performed before concluding salicylate intolerance to rule out more common conditions.

related_conditions.name: Histamine intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Both involve sensitivity to food compounds and can cause similar symptoms (headaches, GI upset, skin reactions). Histamine intolerance results from impaired breakdown of histamine (due to low DAO enzyme activity), while salicylate intolerance reflects difficulty metabolising salicylates. Many people have both; testing and dietary management differ between the two.

related_conditions.name: Amine intolerance (tyramine, phenylethylamine)
related_conditions.explanation: Overlaps with salicylate intolerance in symptom presentation and food sources. Amines (e.g. in aged foods, fermented items) can trigger migraines and neurological symptoms. Some individuals are sensitive to multiple amine and salicylate compounds. Dietary restrictions may overlap.

related_conditions.name: Irritable bowel syndrome (IBS)
related_conditions.explanation: Salicylate intolerance frequently coexists with IBS. Both cause digestive symptoms (bloating, diarrhoea, cramping); salicylate intolerance may be an unrecognised trigger in some IBS patients. Management of both conditions may involve dietary modification.

related_conditions.name: FODMAP intolerance
related_conditions.explanation: Both are non-immune food sensitivities causing GI symptoms. FODMAPs are fermentable carbohydrates; salicylates are plant compounds. Confusion arises because some high-salicylate foods are also high in FODMAPs (e.g. stone fruits, onions). Testing and dietary approaches differ; some people have both.

related_conditions.name: Attention-deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD)
related_conditions.explanation: Salicylate intolerance is anecdotally reported more frequently in children with ADHD. High-salicylate foods may exacerbate hyperactivity or inattention in susceptible individuals. Rigorous evidence is limited, but some families report improvement in ADHD symptoms with a low-salicylate diet.

related_conditions.name: Autism spectrum disorder
related_conditions.explanation: Some people with autism report salicylate sensitivity. Symptoms may manifest as sensory dysregulation, mood changes, or GI upset. Anecdotal evidence suggests dietary modification helps some autistic individuals, though systematic evidence is sparse.

related_conditions.name: Migraines
related_conditions.explanation: Salicylates and related compounds can trigger migraines through vascular and neurochemical mechanisms. People with a history of migraines may be more prone to salicylate intolerance or may find that managing salicylate intake improves headache frequency or severity.

related_conditions.name: Coeliac disease
related_conditions.explanation: Untreated coeliac disease damages the small intestine and impairs nutrient absorption and enzyme function, potentially worsening intolerance to various food compounds including salicylates. Salicylate sensitivity may improve after coeliac disease is diagnosed and managed with a gluten-free diet.

related_conditions.name: Leaky gut syndrome (increased intestinal permeability)
related_conditions.explanation: Increased intestinal permeability may allow larger salicylate molecules or metabolites to cross the intestinal barrier, triggering systemic symptoms. Some theories suggest that reducing salicylate load helps restore gut barrier function; the mechanism is not proven.

strategies.name: Low-salicylate diet (elimination and reintroduction)
strategies.description: Start with a strictly low-salicylate diet for 3–5 days, then systematically reintroduce foods to identify individual tolerance thresholds. Focus on peeled potatoes, white rice, meat, fish, and low-salicylate fruits (pears, bananas). Avoid berries, stone fruits, tomatoes, spices, and processed foods initially. Work with a dietitian to ensure nutritional adequacy and to establish a sustainable long-term diet that includes tolerated foods.
strategies.notes: Effectiveness varies widely between individuals. Some people tolerate moderate salicylate amounts, while others require strict avoidance. The diet is restrictive and nutrient-poor if applied long-term without professional guidance. Individual tolerance thresholds must be established through careful reintroduction.

strategies.name: Rotational diet (varying foods)
strategies.description: Eat different foods on different days (e.g. chicken one day, fish another) to avoid cumulative salicylate load and reduce the likelihood of developing new sensitivities. Rotate low-salicylate vegetables and grains daily. This approach may help maintain dietary variety while managing symptoms.
strategies.notes: Evidence is limited and mostly anecdotal. The mechanism (whether cumulative load or gut habituation) is not clear. May be useful for some individuals but is complex to manage without professional support.

strategies.name: Peeling and cooking methods
strategies.description: Remove skins from potatoes, apples, and pears (salicylates concentrate in skins). Boil rather than bake or fry when possible, as some cooking methods may affect salicylate content. Avoid using salts or spices high in salicylates. Prepare fresh foods rather than relying on processed or pre-spiced items.
strategies.notes: Peeling reduces but does not eliminate salicylates. Heat may slightly reduce salicylate levels, though evidence is mixed. These simple adjustments can help without requiring strict elimination diets.

strategies.name: Antihistamine medications (short-term symptom relief)
strategies.description: Over-the-counter antihistamines (e.g. cetirizine, loratadine) or prescribed H1/H2 antagonists may reduce some symptoms, particularly headaches or flushing, if salicylate intolerance has an histamine component. Use as needed for acute symptoms or preventatively before known trigger meals.
strategies.notes: Efficacy is inconsistent and varies between individuals. Antihistamines address symptoms rather than the underlying issue. Do not use as a substitute for dietary avoidance. Long-term use of antihistamines is not recommended without medical supervision.

strategies.name: DAO enzyme supplements
strategies.description: Over-the-counter DAO (diamine oxidase) supplements are marketed to support histamine breakdown. Taken before meals, these may help if salicylate intolerance overlaps with histamine intolerance. Efficacy is unproven for salicylate-specific symptoms.
strategies.notes: Evidence for DAO supplementation is weak and mostly from histamine intolerance research. Supplements vary widely in quality and potency. Not regulated by most health authorities. May have modest benefit for some individuals but should not replace dietary management.

strategies.name: Gut barrier support supplements (glutamine, zinc, collagen)
strategies.description: Supplements supporting intestinal health and permeability are sometimes recommended alongside dietary changes. Examples include L-glutamine, zinc, bone broth, or collagen peptides. Theoretically, a healthier gut barrier may reduce symptom severity, though direct evidence is lacking.
strategies.notes: Evidence is theoretical and anecdotal. These supplements are generally safe but should be checked for salicylate content (some may be high). Do not use as a substitute for dietary management. Benefits, if any, are likely modest.

strategies.name: Stress management and sleep optimisation
strategies.description: Stress and poor sleep can lower the threshold for food sensitivities and worsen neurological symptoms. Practise relaxation (meditation, deep breathing), maintain consistent sleep schedules, and exercise regularly. These changes may improve overall symptom tolerance.
strategies.notes: Stress and sleep are non-specific but important factors in managing any sensitivity. Improvements may take weeks to become apparent. Combine with dietary management for best results.

strategies.name: Food labelling and ingredient checking
strategies.description: Learn to read food labels and identify high-salicylate ingredients (spices, tomato paste, citric acid, preservatives). Avoid processed foods, sauces, and pre-packaged meals, which often contain high-salicylate flavourings. Stick to fresh, unprocessed foods when possible.
strategies.notes: Essential for managing a low-salicylate diet. Salicylate content is often not listed; familiarisation with common high-salicylate foods and brands is necessary. Online databases and dietitian guidance are valuable resources.

strategies.name: Keeping a detailed symptom and food diary
strategies.description: Log all foods, drinks, supplements, and symptoms (type, timing, severity) daily. Record context (stress, sleep, exercise) as well. Review regularly to identify patterns and refine triggers. Share with healthcare providers to inform diagnosis and management.
strategies.notes: Diaries reveal patterns over time and help distinguish salicylate intolerance from other causes. Accuracy and consistency are critical. Digital tools or apps can simplify logging.

strategies.name: Working with a registered dietitian
strategies.description: A dietitian experienced in food intolerances can design a personalised, nutritionally adequate low-salicylate diet, supervise reintroduction, and help you navigate long-term management. This professional support improves outcomes and ensures nutritional adequacy.
strategies.notes: Professional guidance significantly improves success rates and prevents nutritional deficiencies. Seek a dietitian with specific experience in salicylate intolerance or food sensitivities. May be covered by insurance if referred by a doctor.

things_to_avoid: Strict long-term elimination without professional guidance — can lead to nutritional deficiencies and is often more restrictive than necessary.
Assuming all high-salicylate foods must be avoided indefinitely — individual tolerance varies; systematic reintroduction identifies actual thresholds.
Self-diagnosing salicylate intolerance without ruling out coeliac disease, IBS, histamine intolerance, or other common sensitivities — misdiagnosis can delay proper treatment.
Using antihistamines or supplements as substitutes for dietary management — these address symptoms only and do not address the underlying problem.
Over-relying on food salicylate databases — content varies by source and food freshness; individual tolerance thresholds are more important than absolute salicylate numbers.
Ignoring stress, sleep, and overall health — these factors significantly affect symptom threshold and can worsen sensitivity if neglected.
Introducing too many new foods or supplements at once — makes it impossible to identify which triggers symptoms.
Assuming family members have identical salicylate sensitivities — individual thresholds and symptom types vary widely, even within families.

key_levers.lever: Accurate identification of individual salicylate thresholds through systematic dietary testing
key_levers.why_it_matters: Generic 'low-salicylate' lists are imprecise; identifying your personal tolerance level prevents unnecessary dietary restriction while protecting you from trigger foods. This personalisation is essential for long-term adherence and quality of life.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin a 3–5 day elimination diet, then reintroduce foods one at a time over 2–3 weeks, carefully documenting which trigger symptoms. Work with a dietitian to ensure accuracy and avoid unnecessarily strict restrictions.
key_levers.timeline: Identification typically takes 4–6 weeks; refinement may continue over 2–3 months.

key_levers.lever: Nutritional counselling to ensure a balanced, sustainable diet within salicylate constraints
key_levers.why_it_matters: A restrictive diet can lead to deficiencies in fibre, vitamins, and minerals, worsening overall health and potentially increasing symptom severity. Ensuring nutritional adequacy sustains long-term adherence and health.
key_levers.how_to_start: Consult a registered dietitian to review your current diet, identify nutritional gaps, and add tolerated nutrient-dense foods (e.g. fortified grains, low-salicylate vegetables, lean proteins). Discuss supplementation if needed.
key_levers.timeline: Initial consultation yields a plan; full dietary balance may take 4–8 weeks to achieve.

key_levers.why_it_matters: Chronic stress and sleep deprivation lower the threshold for food sensitivities and trigger or worsen neurological symptoms (headaches, mood changes). Managing these foundational health factors has outsized impact on symptom control.
key_levers.how_to_start: Establish a consistent sleep schedule (7–9 hours nightly), practise 10–15 minutes of daily relaxation (meditation, breathing exercises, yoga), and assess sources of stress. Reduce or delegate high-stress activities where possible.
key_levers.timeline: Sleep improvements appear within 1–2 weeks; stress-related symptom reduction takes 2–4 weeks of consistent practice.

key_levers.lever: Regular physical activity tailored to your tolerance
key_levers.why_it_matters: Exercise reduces stress, improves sleep quality, supports gut health, and may increase digestive enzyme function. These effects enhance overall symptom tolerance and mood.
key_levers.how_to_start: Begin with 20–30 minutes of moderate activity (walking, cycling, swimming) 3–4 times weekly. Avoid intense exercise immediately after eating high-salicylate foods, which may exacerbate digestive symptoms.
key_levers.timeline: Mood and sleep improvements appear within 2–3 weeks; longer-term digestive benefits take 6–8 weeks.

key_levers.lever: Rule out and manage coexisting conditions (coeliac disease, histamine intolerance, FODMAP sensitivity)
key_levers.why_it_matters: Undiagnosed or unmanaged coeliac disease or other food sensitivities can mask or worsen salicylate intolerance symptoms. Addressing all contributors dramatically improves outcomes.
key_levers.how_to_start: Discuss testing for coeliac disease (tissue transglutaminase serology), IgE allergy, and histamine intolerance with your GP or gastroenterologist. If indicated, pursue these investigations before or alongside salicylate management.
key_levers.timeline: Testing and diagnosis typically occur within 2–4 weeks; symptom improvement from managing coexisting conditions appears within 2–8 weeks.

key_levers.lever: Gradual reintroduction and dietary expansion once tolerance is established
key_levers.why_it_matters: Over time, careful reintroduction of previously problematic foods (in controlled quantities) may expand your dietary repertoire, improve nutrition, and reduce the psychological burden of restriction. Some people's tolerance improves with sustained dietary management.
key_levers.how_to_start: After 4–6 weeks on a low-salicylate diet with symptom control, begin introducing previously avoided foods in very small amounts (e.g. one strawberry, a quarter tomato). Wait 48–72 hours between introductions and document tolerance.
key_levers.timeline: Meaningful expansion typically occurs over 3–6 months; some foods may never be tolerated, while others become manageable.

common_mistakes: Starting an overly restrictive diet without professional guidance and then being unable to maintain it due to nutritional or psychological burden — unsustainable diets fail and lead to frustration.
Assuming all high-salicylate foods are permanently forbidden — many people tolerate small amounts; premature elimination can reduce quality of life without improving health.
Neglecting stress, sleep, and general health while focusing only on diet — these factors significantly affect symptom threshold; ignoring them limits the effectiveness of dietary management alone.
Interpreting symptom improvement as 'cured' and then suddenly reintroducing many high-salicylate foods — symptoms often recur, leading to confusion about actual triggers and discouragement.
Self-diagnosing salicylate intolerance and missing other treatable conditions (coeliac disease, histamine intolerance, IBS) — misdiagnosis wastes time and resources.
Using supplements or medications as a primary strategy instead of addressing diet — these provide only temporary symptom relief and do not resolve the underlying issue.
Sharing identical dietary restrictions with family members based on one person's sensitivities — individual tolerance thresholds vary; unnecessarily restricting others' diets can breed resentment.
Waiting months for symptom improvement before adjusting the diet — if symptoms do not improve within 2–3 weeks of strict elimination, the diagnosis is likely incorrect; seek alternative explanations.

specialists.role: Essential first point of contact. Provides expert nutritional assessment, designs personalised low-salicylate diets, supervises elimination and reintroduction, and ensures long-term nutritional adequacy. Most effective practitioners for this condition.
specialists.notes: Many insurance plans cover dietitian referrals if made by a physician. Look for dietitians with specific experience in food intolerances, gastrointestinal conditions, or integrative nutrition. In Australia and New Zealand, dietitians are more likely to have formal training in salicylate intolerance.

specialists.role: Evaluates GI symptoms, rules out coeliac disease, IBD, IBS, and other digestive disorders that may mimic or coexist with salicylate intolerance. Coordinates investigation and referral to dietitian.
specialists.notes: May be sceptical of salicylate intolerance if it is not yet widely recognised in your region. Best approached with documentation of symptom patterns and dietary trials. A gastroenterologist experienced in food sensitivities is preferable.

specialists.role: Performs testing for IgE-mediated food allergy and helps distinguish true allergy from intolerance. Negative allergy testing supports salicylate intolerance diagnosis.
specialists.notes: Most allergists focus on classic IgE allergy and may have limited experience with non-immune food sensitivities. Useful for ruling out allergy rather than confirming intolerance.

specialists.role: Often more familiar with salicylate intolerance and other food sensitivities. Provides holistic evaluation, coordinates dietary intervention, and may suggest gut-supportive supplements. May order non-standard tests (e.g. PST enzyme activity).
specialists.notes: Quality and training vary widely; not all practitioners are formally regulated. Seek practitioners with credentials (MD, DO, ND) and specific experience in salicylate intolerance. Be cautious of excessive supplement recommendations or unproven testing.

specialists.type: Primary Care Physician (GP or General Practitioner)
specialists.role: Initial evaluation of symptoms, ruling out serious underlying conditions, and referral to appropriate specialists. Coordinates investigation and provides medical oversight.
specialists.notes: Many primary care physicians have limited awareness of salicylate intolerance. Bringing documented symptom and food patterns significantly helps. Request a referral to a dietitian or gastroenterologist if your GP is unfamiliar with the condition.

specialists.role: For children with suspected salicylate intolerance, especially those with ADHD, autism, or developmental concerns. Evaluates behavioural and neurological symptoms and coordinates dietary trial.
specialists.notes: Salicylate intolerance is more commonly recognised in paediatric settings in Australia and New Zealand. Some developmental paediatricians routinely screen for this condition.

patient_communities.name: Salicylate Intolerance Support Australia (SISA)
patient_communities.notes: The most active and established English-language community focused specifically on salicylate intolerance. Provides food lists, recipes, and peer support. Based in Australia but inclusive of international members.

patient_communities.name: Failsafe Diet Facebook Group
patient_communities.notes: Large, active community centred on the Failsafe (Free From Additives, Salicylates, Amines, Monosodium Glutamate, and Flavour Enhancers) diet. Includes salicylate intolerance as a subset. Provides recipes, peer support, and shared experiences.

patient_communities.name: Food Intolerance Network (Australia)
patient_communities.notes: Comprehensive Australian resource covering salicylate intolerance, other food sensitivities, and dietary management. Includes food lists, recipes, and evidence-based information.

patient_communities.name: IBS and Food Sensitivities subreddit
patient_communities.notes: Broader communities that include salicylate intolerance discussions. Good for peer support and shared experiences, though moderation and evidence quality vary.

patient_communities.name: Low Histamine/Salicylate Facebook Groups
patient_communities.url: Search 'Low Histamine' or 'Low Salicylate' on Facebook
patient_communities.notes: Multiple active groups combining histamine and salicylate intolerance support. Useful if you suspect overlapping sensitivities. Vary in size and moderator expertise.

learning_resources.title: Salicylates and Other Failing Foods in the Diagnosis and Management of Pervasive Developmental Disorder
learning_resources.description: A foundational research paper examining salicylate intolerance in autism and ADHD. Provides evidence for dietary intervention and detailed information on salicylate mechanisms. Somewhat technical but highly credible.

learning_resources.title: The Failsafe Handbook
learning_resources.description: A comprehensive guide to the Failsafe diet (salicylates, amines, additives, glutamates) written by a leading researcher in food intolerance. Includes extensive food lists, recipes, and case studies. Practical and evidence-informed.

learning_resources.title: Salicylate Intolerance and the Lowering of the Histamine Threshold
learning_resources.author_or_source: Various integrative and functional medicine websites
learning_resources.description: Explanations of the possible link between salicylate intolerance and histamine sensitivity. Helps readers understand overlap between these conditions and why dual dietary management may be necessary.

learning_resources.title: Food Intolerance Network website (Australia)
learning_resources.description: Extensive, evidence-based information on salicylate intolerance, other food sensitivities, and dietary management. Includes food lists, recipes, support group links, and peer-reviewed research summaries. Excellent starting point.

learning_resources.title: Salicylate Content of Common Foods
learning_resources.author_or_source: Various sources (Fedewa, Curtin University, Food Intolerance Network)
learning_resources.description: Searchable databases and lists ranking foods by salicylate content (low, medium, high). Essential practical tool for dietary planning. Content varies between sources due to food variability.

learning_resources.title: Diagnosis and Management of Food Sensitivity in Autism
learning_resources.description: Reviews evidence for food sensitivities (including salicylates) in autism spectrum disorder and outlines diagnostic and management approaches. Credible but mixed-evidence field.

learning_resources.title: The Sensitive Gut: Understanding and Healing IBS and Other Functional Digestive Conditions
learning_resources.description: A comprehensive guide to functional gastrointestinal disorders including food sensitivities. Includes chapters on salicylate and other intolerances, with practical management strategies. Evidence-based and accessible.

learning_resources.title: Integrative Nutrition: Feed Your Hunger for Health and Happiness (Chapters on Food Sensitivities)
learning_resources.author_or_source: Katz, D.L. or similar nutrition texts
learning_resources.description: General nutrition books addressing food sensitivities and individual dietary variation. Broader context for understanding salicylate intolerance within whole-diet and lifestyle approach.

learning_resources.title: Registered Dietitian Nutritionist Referral Directory
learning_resources.author_or_source: Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics (USA) or Dietitians Australia
learning_resources.description: Searchable directories to find registered dietitians with specialisation in food sensitivities and gastrointestinal conditions. Essential for locating qualified professional support.

learning_resources.title: Gut Health and Salicylate Intolerance: Mechanisms and Integrative Approaches
learning_resources.description: Discusses possible mechanisms linking gut health, intestinal permeability, and salicylate intolerance. Helps explain why stress, sleep, and overall wellness affect symptom tolerance. Information ranges from evidence-based to speculative.

sections.relevance_signal: Symptoms after **fresh herbs, berries, tomatoes, and spices** — but not after fermented foods specifically. Overlaps with histamine symptoms but triggered by fresh rather than aged foods.
